ggerganov/llama.cpp#20308.body:
### Name and Version

version: 8241 (62b8143ad)
built with Clang 19.1.5 for Windows x86_64

### Operating systems

Windows

### GGML backends

CUDA

### Hardware

RTX 5090 + RTX 4090 + RPC on a NVIDIA GB10 based machine (Asus GX10, similar to a DGX Spark)

The issue is not specific to this hardware and the CUDA / RPC backends, it can be reproduced with any GPU.

### Models

lmstudio-community/Qwen3.5-397B-A17B-GGUF:Q4_K_M

The issue is not model related, it can be reproduced with any model.

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

When using values higher than 4095 MiB for the `--fit-target` on Windows, an integer overflow occurs. As a result, you can't set a target higher than 4095 MiB on Windows.

Since the issue is `fit` related, it can't be reproduced with `-fit off`

For example, running with `--fit-target 4095,4096,4097` for my 3 backends results in this output during initialization:

```
0.02.832.376 I common_init_result: fitting params to device memory, for bugs during this step try to reproduce them with -fit off, or provide --verbose logs if the bug only occurs with -fit on
0.03.911.357 I llama_params_fit_impl: projected memory use with initial parameters [MiB]:
0.03.911.365 I llama_params_fit_impl:   - RPC0 (gx10-bb36:50052)         : 122502 total, 164575 used, -46590 free vs. target of   4095
0.03.911.366 I llama_params_fit_impl:   - CUDA0 (NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090):  32606 total,  43674 used, -12881 free vs. target of      0
0.03.911.366 I llama_params_fit_impl:   - CUDA1 (NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090):  24563 total,  30862 used,  -7958 free vs. target of      1
0.03.911.367 I llama_params_fit_impl: projected to use 239111 MiB of device memory vs. 171682 MiB of free device memory
0.03.911.367 I llama_params_fit_impl: cannot meet free memory targets on all devices, need to use 71525 MiB less in total
0.03.911.367 I llama_params_fit_impl: context size set by user to 262144 -> no change
0.04.322.483 I llama_params_fit_impl: with only dense weights in device memory there is a total surplus of 148469 MiB
0.04.804.938 I llama_params_fit_impl: filling dense-only layers back-to-front:
0.05.264.416 I llama_params_fit_impl:   - CUDA1 (NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090): 61 layers,  18938 MiB used,   3965 MiB free
0.05.264.424 I llama_params_fit_impl:   - CUDA0 (NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090):  0 layers,   2840 MiB used,  27952 MiB free
0.05.264.425 I llama_params_fit_impl:   - RPC0 (gx10-bb36:50052)         :  0 layers,      0 MiB used, 117985 MiB free
0.05.264.426 I llama_params_fit_impl: converting dense-only layers to full layers and filling them front-to-back with overflow to next device/system memory:
0.09.141.830 I llama_params_fit_impl:   - RPC0 (gx10-bb36:50052)         : 29 layers ( 1 overflowing), 113619 MiB used,   4366 MiB free
0.15.511.768 I llama_params_fit_impl:   - CUDA0 (NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090):  7 layers ( 1 overflowing),  29761 MiB used,   1031 MiB free
0.18.824.381 I llama_params_fit_impl:   - CUDA1 (NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090): 25 layers (21 overflowing),  22644 MiB used,    259 MiB free
0.18.824.391 I llama_params_fit: successfully fit params to free device memory
```

As you can see, `4096` results in an actual target of `0` and  and `4097` results in an actual target of `1`.

Interrupting the process confirms that it was actually not using the correct targets for those two devices:

```
8.21.980.896 I srv    operator(): operator(): cleaning up before exit...
8.21.984.092 I llama_memory_breakdown_print: | memory breakdown [MiB]     |  total   free      self    model   context   compute    unaccounted |
8.21.984.095 I llama_memory_breakdown_print: |   - RPC0 (gx10-bb36:50052) | 122502 = 3130 + (113619 = 108831 +    3948 +     840) +        5752 |
8.21.984.096 I llama_memory_breakdown_print: |   - CUDA0 (RTX 5090)       |  32606 =  795 + ( 29761 =  25813 +    1106 +    2840) +        2050 |
8.21.984.096 I llama_memory_breakdown_print: |   - CUDA1 (RTX 4090)       |  24563 =    0 + ( 22652 =  18773 +    3370 +     509) +        1910 |
8.21.984.097 I llama_memory_breakdown_print: |   - Host                   |                   76544 =  76016 +       0 +     528                |
```

As far as I can tell, this overflow is not a security issue.

I asked Claude Opus 4.6 high reasoning to look into it, and this is what it came up with:

# `--fit-target` values >= 4096 silently overflow to 0 on Windows

## Summary

The `--fit-target` (aka `-fitt`) parameter silently overflows to 0 for values >= 4096 MiB on Windows due to a 32-bit integer overflow in the argument parsing code.

## Reproduction

On Windows (any 64-bit version):

```
llama-cli -m model.gguf -fit on -fitt 4096
```

The margin is silently set to 0 instead of 4096 MiB. Values below 4096 work correctly.

## Root Cause

In `common/arg.cpp`, the parsing code converts MiB to bytes using:

```cpp
// line 2430 (single value):
std::fill(params.fit_params_target.begin(), params.fit_params_target.end(),
    std::stoul(split_arg[0]) * 1024*1024);

// line 2434 (multi-device):
params.fit_params_target[i] = std::stoul(split_arg[i]) * 1024*1024;
```

`std::stoul()` returns `unsigned long`, which is **32-bit on Windows** (even on 64-bit systems — this is a well-known MSVC/Windows ABI property). The multiplication `4096 * 1024 * 1024 = 2^32` overflows a 32-bit unsigned integer to exactly 0. Values below 4096 produce results < 2^32 and work fine.

The storage type `std::vector<size_t>` (in `common/common.h:389`) is 64-bit and can hold the value — the overflow happens before the assignment.

## Suggested Fix

Replace `std::stoul` with `std::stoull` (returns `unsigned long long`, guaranteed 64-bit on all platforms):

```cpp
// line 2430:
std::fill(params.fit_params_target.begin(), params.fit_params_target.end(),
    std::stoull(split_arg[0]) * 1024*1024);

// line 2434:
params.fit_params_target[i] = std::stoull(split_arg[i]) * 1024*1024;
```

## Environment

- Windows 11 (64-bit), MSVC toolchain
- `unsigned long` is 32-bit on Windows per the MSVC ABI
- Issue does not reproduce on Linux/macOS where `unsigned long` is 64-bit

### Name and Version

version: 8254 (107d59995)

### Operating systems

Linux

### Which llama.cpp modules do you know to be affected?

llama-server

### Command line

```shell
llama-server -m Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-IQ4_XS.gguf
```

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

When using the `/completion` endpoint, the console gets spammed by "No parser definition detected, assuming pure content parser.", without any line return. It's mostly a logging issue that doesn't cause any generation issue.

### First Bad Commit

Introduced by https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/commit/566059a26b0ce8faec4ea053605719d399c64cc5 (autoparser)

### Relevant log output

_No response_

```
version: 8269 (0cd4f4720)
built with GNU 13.3.0 for Linux aarch64
```

### Operating systems

Linux

### Which llama.cpp modules do you know to be affected?

llama-server

### Command line

```shell
# Server
./llama-server --gpt-oss-120b-default --host 0.0.0.0


# Client
curl http://10.126.190.221:8013/v1/chat/completions \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -H "x-api-key: 123456" \
    -d '{
        "model": "ggml-org/gpt-oss-120b-GGUF",
        "messages": [
            {
                "role": "system",
                "content": "You are a helpful assistant that generates JSON according to a given JSON Schema."
            },
            {
                "role": "user",
                "content": "Generate a JSON object that conforms to the following JSON Schema: {\"type\": \"object\", \"properties\": {\"name\": {\"type\": \"string\"}, \"age\": {\"type\": \"integer\"}}, \"required\": [\"name\", \"age\"]}"
            }
        ],
        "response_format": {
            "type": "json_object",
            "json_schema": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                    "name": {"type": "string"},
                    "age": {"type": "integer"}
                },
                "required": ["name", "age"]
            }
        }
    }'
```

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

When trying to generate structured output by specifying a json schema, I get 500 server errors. This is happening on a DGX Spark running Ubuntu.

Compile the relevant version of llama.cpp and run the two commands above, substituting the IP address.

The output on the client side is:

```
{"error":{"code":500,"message":"Failed to parse input at pos 416: <|start|>assistant<|channel|>final<|message|>{\n  \"name\": \"John Doe\",\n  \"age\": 30\n}","type":"server_error"}}
```

ggerganov/llama.cpp#20359.body:
### Name and Version

```
version: 1 (6c770d1)
built with GNU 13.3.0 for Linux aarch64
```

Built from latest master commit `6c770d1` ("Reduce level of content parser warning message #20347") on 2026-03-10.

### Operating systems

Linux

### GGML backends

CUDA

### Hardware

NVIDIA DGX Spark GB10 (ARM64, 128GB unified memory)

### Models

- Qwen3-14B-Q8_0.gguf
- Qwen3-30B-A3B-Q8_0.gguf

Both models produce the same corruption. Issue is not model-specific.

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

### Summary

When llama-server receives a request with a large system prompt and many tool definitions, the `arguments` field in tool call responses contains invalid JSON with mixed single and double quotes. The same request with a small/simple payload returns valid JSON.

### Details

The `arguments` field in the response contains:

```json
{"name": "Front Door Lock', 'domain': 'lock"}
```

Instead of the expected valid JSON:

```json
{"name": "Front Door Lock", "domain": "lock"}
```

The raw HTTP response bytes confirm the corruption originates from llama-server — no SDK or middleware is involved:

```
b'arguments":"{\\"name\\": \\"Front Door Lock\', \'domain\': \'lock\\"}'
```

The `\\"` (escaped double quote) is correct for the first field, but after the first value, the quotes switch to `\'` (escaped single quote). This produces invalid JSON that cannot be parsed by standard JSON parsers.

### Key finding

The corruption **only occurs with large/complex payloads**. A minimal request with the same model, same tools schema, and same user message returns perfectly valid JSON. The trigger appears to be the combination of a long system prompt (~5000+ chars) with multiple tool definitions (~20+ tools).

### llama-server flags

```
--jinja
--chat-template-kwargs '{"enable_thinking":false,"reasoning_effort":"low"}'
--reasoning-format deepseek
-fa on
--temp 0.0
```

### Steps to reproduce

1. Start llama-server with the flags above and a Qwen3 model.

2. **Small payload — works correctly:**

```bash
curl -s http://localhost:8081/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
  "model": "qwen3",
  "temperature": 0.0,
  "stream": false,
  "messages": [
    {"role": "system", "content": "You are a smart home assistant."},
    {"role": "user", "content": "Lock the front door."}
  ],
  "tools": [{
    "type": "function",
    "function": {
      "name": "HassTurnOn",
      "description": "Turns on a device.",
      "parameters": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "name": {"type": "string"},
          "domain": {"type": "string"}
        },
        "required": ["name", "domain"]
      }
    }
  }]
}'
```

Response arguments: `{"domain": "lock", "name": "Front Door Lock"}` — **valid JSON**.

3. **Large payload — corrupted:**

Send the same request but with a large system prompt (~5000+ chars containing device context, scripts, multiple instruction sections) and ~20+ tool definitions. The attached `diag_payload.json` file is the exact payload that triggers the bug.

```bash
curl -s http://localhost:8081/v1/chat/completions \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d @diag_payload.json | python3 -c "
import sys, json
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
for choice in data.get('choices', []):
    msg = choice.get('message', {})
    for tc in msg.get('tool_calls', []):
        print('arguments:', repr(tc['function']['arguments']))
"
```

Response arguments: `'{"name": "Front Door Lock\', \'domain\': \'lock"}'` — **invalid JSON with mixed quotes**.

4. This reproduces with both `stream: true` and `stream: false`.

5. This reproduces across multiple models (Qwen3-14B, Qwen3-30B-A3B).

### Impact

This breaks any OpenAI-compatible client that calls `json.loads()` on tool call arguments, which is the standard approach. This is particularly impactful for Home Assistant integrations that use llama-server for local smart home voice control, where every device command requires a tool call with valid JSON arguments.


### First Bad Commit

_No response_

### Relevant log output

Raw HTTP response bytes from llama-server (captured via curl, no SDK involved):

```
b'arguments":"{\\"name\\": \\"Front Door Lock\', \'domain\': \'lock\\"}'
```

Expected:

```
b'arguments":"{\\"name\\": \\"Front Door Lock\\", \\"domain\\": \\"lock\\"}'
```

[diag_payload.json](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25879886/diag_payload.json)
ggerganov/llama.cpp#20359.title: Streamed and non-streamed tool call arguments contain invalid JSON (mixed single/double quotes) with large payloads

ggerganov/llama.cpp#20414.body:
### Name and Version

$ ./build/bin/llama-cli --version
ggml_cuda_init: found 2 CUDA devices:
  Device 0: NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Max-Q Workstation Edition, compute capability 12.0, VMM: yes
  Device 1: NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Max-Q Workstation Edition, compute capability 12.0, VMM: yes
version: 8245 (d417bc43d)
built with GNU 15.2.1 for Linux x86_64

### Operating systems

Linux

### Which llama.cpp modules do you know to be affected?

llama-server

### Command line

```shell
CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0 ./build/bin/llama-server --n-cpu-moe 999 --threads 48 --ctx-size 150000 --flash-attn on --model /mnt/srv/snowdrift/ggml/Qwen3.5-35B-A3B/Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-BF16.gguf --jinja
```

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

I've recently updated my [Qwen3.5 MoE quantizations](https://huggingface.co/AesSedai/Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-GGUF) to use the [fused gate + up tensor PR](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/pull/19139). 

I've seen two reports that the fused gate + up model breaks the `--n-cpu-moe` flag but `--fit` works. I tested locally with the `--n-cpu-moe` flag and got the same issue.

Entirely speculation right now, but I think it might be that the `--n-cpu-moe` flag is calculating sizes incorrectly because the tensor name has changed post-fusion? I will try to investigate more later today.

_No response_

ggerganov/llama.cpp#20436.body:
## Summary

The fused Gated Delta Net (GDN) kernel has a memory access anti-pattern across all three backends (Metal, CUDA, CPU). The `[S_v, S_v]` state matrix is stored row-major (dim 0 contiguous), but the kernel reads/writes it column-wise, resulting in poor cache utilization and measurable performance regression at certain model sizes.

## Root Cause

```metal
// Metal kernel — current code
s_ptr = base + i20;              // i20 = column index
ls[j] = s_ptr[is * S_v];        // stride S_v = 128 floats = 512 bytes between reads
```

A 32-thread SIMD group issues 32 loads spaced 512 bytes apart. Each load pulls a separate 128-byte cache line but uses only 4 bytes from it — **3% cache utilization**. The same pattern exists in:

- **CUDA**: `curr_state[i * S_v + col]` — strided access across warps (`gated_delta_net.cu`)
- **CPU**: `s_out[i * S_v]` — non-sequential memory access

## Proposed Fix

Transpose the indexing so threads read with minimal stride:

```metal
// Metal kernel — fixed
s_ptr = base + i20 * S_v;       // row base
ls[j] = s_ptr[is];              // stride NSG between SIMD threads
```

### Cache utilization before/after

|  | Before (stride S_v) | After (stride 1 or NSG) |
|--|---------------------|------------------------|
| **Metal** (per SIMD load) | 32 cache lines, 3% utilization | 4 cache lines, 25% per load (100% amortized across j=0..NSG-1 unrolled loop) |
| **CUDA** (per warp load) | S_v/warp_size cache lines, strided | 1 cache line, 100% utilization |

On Metal with NSG=4, `is = tx*NSG + j`, so the j=0 SIMD load reads positions 0, 4, 8, ..., 124 (stride 4, spanning 4 cache lines). But across the full unrolled loop (j=0..3), positions 0–127 are all covered, so amortized utilization approaches ~100% if cache lines are retained between iterations. On CUDA, `i = r * warp_size + lane`, so lanes 0–31 read truly consecutive addresses — one cache line, 100% utilization.

## Benchmark Evidence

Tested on Apple M4 Max (128 GB), macOS, Metal backend. Commit `d28961d81` (PR #20340).

Single-token generation latency (Qwen3.5 Q4_K_M, prompt: "What time is it?"):

| Model | Unfused baseline | Fused (d28961d81) | Delta |
|-------|-----------------|-------------------|-------|
| Qwen3.5-0.8B | 525ms | 403–434ms | -17–23% faster |
| Qwen3.5-9B | 1,905ms | 2,637–2,650ms | **+39% slower** |
| Qwen3.5-27B | 41,634ms | 14,776–14,893ms | -64% faster |

## Why 9B is the regression sweet spot

All three models have `S_v = 128` (= `ssm_d_inner / ssm_dt_rank`) and `NSG = 4`, using `kernel_gated_delta_net_f32_4`:

| Model | ssm_d_inner | ssm_dt_rank (num_v_heads) | S_v | Recurrent layers | State per layer |
|-------|-------------|--------------------------|-----|------------------|-----------------|
| 0.8B  | 2048 | 16 | 128 | 18 (24 × 3/4) | 1 MB |
| 9B    | 4096 | 32 | 128 | 24 (32 × 3/4) | 2 MB |
| 27B   | 6144 | 48 | 128 | 48 (64 × 3/4) | 3 MB |

(Recurrent layers = `block_count × 3/4`, since `full_attention_interval = 4`)

- **0.8B** (1 MB/layer): Fits in L2 cache, masking the bad access pattern
- **9B** (2 MB/layer, 24 recurrent layers): Spills L2, amplifying the cache utilization penalty
- **27B** (3 MB/layer): Also spills, but the fused kernel still wins because unfused path's many kernel launches are costlier at this scale

## Model GGUF Metadata

```
# All three models:
ssm.state_size = 128, ssm.group_count = 16, ssm.conv_kernel = 4

# 0.8B: block_count=24, head_count=8, head_count_kv=2, ssm.inner_size=2048, ssm.time_step_rank=16
# 9B:   block_count=32, head_count=16, head_count_kv=4, ssm.inner_size=4096, ssm.time_step_rank=32
# 27B:  block_count=64, head_count=24, head_count_kv=4, ssm.inner_size=6144, ssm.time_step_rank=48
```

## Affected Code

- `ggml/src/ggml-metal/ggml-metal.metal` — `kernel_gated_delta_net_impl` template
- `ggml/src/ggml-cuda/gated_delta_net.cu` — CUDA kernel state access
- `ggml/src/ggml-cpu/ops.cpp` — `ggml_compute_forward_gated_delta_net` CPU impl
ggerganov/llama.cpp#20436.title: Fused GDN kernel: column-wise state access causes cache thrashing on Metal/CUDA/CPU

### Name and Version

```
$ llama-server --version
version: 8324 (983df142)
built with GNU 15.2.1 for Linux x86_64
```

### Operating systems

Linux

### Which llama.cpp modules do you know to be affected?

llama-server

### Command line

```shell
llama-server --no-models-autoload --models-preset /models/models.in
```

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

The model selector in the web UI now selects the first model that loads and does not allow changing to other loaded models. The only way to change the model now is by unloading unwanted models and only loading the desired model.

 It can be reproduced by starting the server with a `models.ini`, loading multiple models and trying to select another loaded model.

### First Bad Commit

I don't know the specific commit, but with the following version the model selector worked correctly:
```
$ llama-server --version
[...]
version: 8256 (f76565db)
built with GNU 15.2.1 for Linux x86_64
```

My guess would be that https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/pull/20403 broke it.

### Relevant log output

This is a WebUI issue and there are no error logged in the JavaScript console.

ggerganov/llama.cpp#20524.body:
The Vulkan container image builds have been failing for the last few days.

These are the jobs that have failed so far:

- https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/actions/runs/23036627015/job/66906129376
- https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/actions/runs/22987035100/job/66739587740
- https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/actions/runs/22937218234/job/66571182057
- https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/actions/runs/22887559149/job/66403648665

This appears caused by Python dependencies being unable to satisfy the constraint `torch~=2.6.0`.

I have tested bumping the requirement to `torch~=2.9.0` in `requirements/requirements-convert_hf_to_gguf.txt`, and the Vulkan image builds successfully. However, I don't suppose that this bump can be done without affecting something else due to breaking changes in PyTorch. Since the other container images are not failing to build with the same requirements, perhaps there is a simpler solution that would address this.
ggerganov/llama.cpp#20524.title: CI: Vulkan container image builds failing

### Name and Version

```bash
version: 8334 (463b6a963)
built with GNU 13.3.0 for Linux aarch64
```

### Operating systems

Linux

### Which llama.cpp modules do you know to be affected?

llama-server

### Command line

```shell
llama-server --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8033 --log-colors on --log-prefix --log-timestamps -ngl 999 --threads -1 --no-mmap --mlock --direct-io --cache-ram 0 -hf ggml-org/gpt-oss-120b-GGUF --jinja --flash-attn 1 --ubatch-size 2048 --batch-size 2048 --temp 1.0 --min-p 0.01 --top-p 1.0 --top-k 0.0 --spec-type ngram-mod --spec-ngram-size-n 24 --draft-min 48 --draft-max 64 --chat-template-kwargs '{"reasoning_effort": "low"}'
```

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

# Summary

Starting the last few days I've been getting server-500 error messages `Failed to parse input at pos` while running the Aider Polyglot eval. I had difficulty trying to find a minimal reproducible test case, it turned out to be a sequence of steps that causes the issue. It appears to me that this is some sort of slot prompt cache corruption that the new AutoParser uncovered. All of my tests were run on a DGX Spark.

I am still working to find the root cause, but decided to post my findings in case someone else can shortcut me to the fix.

# Error samples
```
567.38.283.954 W srv    operator(): got exception: {"error":{"code":500,"message":"Failed to parse input at pos 0: ...<|message|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>analysis<|message|>We need to produce a summary per instructions. The user wants a brief summary of the partial conversation, focusing more on recent messages. Must include function names, libraries, packages, filenames referenced. No code fences. Must start with \"I asked you...\". Write as user, referring to assistant as you. Summarize: initial request about Zebra Puzzle, assistant gave simple solve implementation in zebra_puzzle.cpp with namespace and solve returning Solution. User said updated files. Assistant responded \"Ok.\" Then user posted build output showing compile errors: solve not a member of zebra_puzzle. Assistant then gave header file with struct Solution and declaration of solve. Need to include that. Also mention files: zebra_puzzle.cpp, zebra_puzzle.h, zebra_puzzle_test.cpp, CMake, etc. Mention standard library <string>. No code fences. Provide new paragraph when topic changes. Let's craft.<|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>final<|message|>I asked you to implement the Zebra Puzzle solution, so you added a zebra_puzzle.cpp file that defines a zebra_puzzle::solve() function returning a Solution object with the hard‑coded answers “Norwegian” and “Japanese”.  \n\nI then told you I had updated the source files, and you replied briefly that you noted the change.  \n\nAfter that I ran the CMake build and posted the compilation output, which showed that zebra_puzzle_test.cpp could not find a solve function in the zebra_puzzle namespace. To fix the errors you provided a new zebra_puzzle.h header that declares the Solution struct (with std::string members drinksWater and ownsZebra) and the solve() function prototype inside the zebra_puzzle namespace, using the standard <string> library.","type":"server_error"}}
45.27.987.355 W srv    operator(): got exception: {"error":{"code":500,"message":"Failed to parse input at pos 0: ...\n<|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>analysis<|message|>We need summary.<|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>final<|message|>I asked you to implement parallel letter‑frequency counting in C++. You first gave me a header **parallel_letter_frequency.h** declaring `std::array<std::size_t, 26> count_letter_frequencies(const std::vector<std::string>& texts);` and a source file **parallel_letter_frequency.cpp** that uses `<algorithm>`, `<execution>`, and helper functions `detail::count_in_string` and `detail::combine_counts` to perform a parallel `std::transform_reduce`.  \n\nLater you pointed out a compilation issue: the header was missing `<cstddef>`. You responded with an updated **parallel_letter_frequency.h** that now includes `<cstddef>` along with `<array>`, `<string>`, and `<vector>`. The function declaration remains the same, and the namespace and include guards are unchanged.","type":"server_error"}}
3.36.810.109 W srv    operator(): got exception: {"error":{"code":500,"message":"Failed to parse input at pos 0: assistant…\n\n<|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>analysis<|message|>We need to produce summary.<|end|><|start|>assistant<|channel|>final<|message|>I asked you to implement a 0/1 knapsack solution. You created **knapsack.h** with the `Item` struct (fields `weight` and `value`) and declared the function `int maxValue(const std::vector<Item>& items, int capacity);` inside the `knapsack` namespace. In **knapsack.cpp** you defined `maxValue` using a one‑dimensional DP array, handling edge cases, skipping items heavier than the capacity, and returning the best achievable value.\n\nLater I told you I had updated the files and pointed out an error marker at line 25 of **knapsack.h** where the `maxValue` declaration appears. You responded by reposting the corrected **knapsack.h** content, confirming the declaration and surrounding documentation remain unchanged.","type":"server_error"}}
3.16.010.983 W srv    operator(): got exception: {"error":{"code":500,"message":"Failed to parse input at pos 55: <|start|>final<|channel|>final<|message|>I asked you to implement a 0/1 knapsack solution and you provided the header **knapsack.h** defining the `knapsack::Item` struct and the function `int maxValue(const std::vector<Item>& items, int capacity);`, along with documentation comments. You also gave the implementation in **knapsack.cpp**, using a one‑dimensional DP array to compute the maximum value without exceeding the capacity.\n\nLater I told you I had updated the files and pointed out an error marker at line 25 in **knapsack.h** where the declaration of `maxValue` appears. You responded by reposting the corrected **knapsack.h** file, ensuring the function declaration is properly placed and the file compiles.","type":"server_error"}}

```

# Findings:
- Error **does not** occur with `--no-cache-prompt`
- Error **does** repeat with same prompt until cache in best match slot is replaced
- Error **does not** occur on same prompt with `--slot-save-path` and `/slots/X?action=erase` api call
- Error **does** occur with ggml-org and unsloth gpt-oss-120b, and all combinations of their chat templates
- Error **does** occur with gpt-oss-120b medium and low thinking
- Error **does** occur with ggml-org gpt-oss-20b (medium thinking only)
- Error **does** occur with and without speculative decoding (ngram-mod)

# Steps to Recreate

In an attempt to nail down the exact call I added temporary logging of the json message posted to `/v1/chat/completions`. I don't recommend this for production and there might be a better way, but this is what I used to recreate.

<details>
<summary>Danger prompt logging</summary>

Logging

**NOTE:** Only apply this patch if you want to create your own replay logs. I've attached the logs I've created using this method. And the patch is not needed for playback.

**`prompt-log.patch`**
```diff
diff --git a/tools/server/server-context.cpp b/tools/server/server-context.cpp
index b4373c101..eee06d542 100644
--- a/tools/server/server-context.cpp
+++ b/tools/server/server-context.cpp
@@ -3651,6 +3651,7 @@ void server_routes::init_routes() {
         auto res = create_response();
         std::vector<raw_buffer> files;
         json body = json::parse(req.body);
+        SRV_INF("Request: %s\n", body.dump().c_str());
         json body_parsed = oaicompat_chat_params_parse(
             body,
             meta->chat_params,
```
And apply it with:
```bash
git apply prompt-log.patch
```

</details>

Using a log file created with the above method, we can spit out the sequence of prompts with the following:
**`replayLog.sh`**
```bash
#!/bin/bash

FAIL_LOG="failLog.log"

# Extract all of the lines from the log that start with this header (note: log creator requires prompt-log.patch to be applied)
PROMPTS=$(sed -n 's/^[^srv]*srv    operator(): Request: //p' $FAIL_LOG)

while IFS= read -r line; do
  echo -e "\n\e[31m*** PROMPT ***\e[0m"
  echo "$line"
  echo -e "\n\e[34m*** RESPONSE ***\e[0m"
  echo "$line" | curl -s --fail-with-body -X POST http://localhost:8033/v1/chat/completions -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d @-
  if [[ "$?" -ne 0 ]]; then
    echo -e "\n\nExiting due to server error...\n\n"
    break
  fi
  echo ""
done <<< "$PROMPTS"
```
**NOTE:** Be sure to pipe all of the llama-server output to file by appending `&> ~/failLog.log`

I will attach my entire log file which can be used with the above script to recreate the error seen.


### First Bad Commit

Errors only started appearing after 566059a (only because of additional error checking present). And appears sporadically at different points through later commits (any small change to the caching or the parsing seems to affect the manifestation).

### Relevant log output

Logs that I used to recreate the issue with the above script:

[failLog.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25987669/failLog.log)
ggerganov/llama.cpp#20532.title: Misc. bug: Server 500 error (gpt-oss, AutoParser, cache)

ggerganov/llama.cpp#20547.body:
### Prerequisites

- [x] I am running the latest code. Mention the version if possible as well.
- [x] I carefully followed the [README.md](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/blob/master/README.md).
- [x] I searched using keywords relevant to my issue to make sure that I am creating a new issue that is not already open (or closed).
- [x] I reviewed the [Discussions](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/discussions), and have a new and useful enhancement to share.

### Feature Description

## Problem Description
When running `llama-server` in a single-model mode, the Web UI provides a very useful "Model Information" button. This view shows critical metadata like the GGUF file path, context size,  the full `chat_template` and some more information.

However, in **Router Mode**, this information is currently inaccessible through the UI. While the router lists available models with "Add to Favorites" and "Load/Unload" buttons, there is no way to inspect the model's internal parameters without checking the terminal logs or querying the API manually.

## Suggested Enhancement
Add a third button (e.g., an "Info" or "List" icon) next to the "Load/Unload" button for each model in the router list. 



### Motivation

For users running local LLMs it is crucial to quickly verify:
1. The **Context Window** to avoid truncation errors.
2. The **Chat Template** (Jinja) to ensure the local IDE is sending correctly formatted prompts.
3. The **Quantization level** to balance performance vs. quality.

Having this at a glance in the Router UI would significantly improve the experience for local-first AI setups.

### Possible Implementation

- **Visibility:** The button should ideally be visible for loaded models (as the metadata is already in memory).
- **Functionality:** Clicking the button should open the same "Model Information" overlay/modal that is already implemented in the non-router UI.
- Since the logic for the 'Model Information' modal is already present in the single-model view, it could likely be reused by passing the metadata from the respective model object in the router's model list."
ggerganov/llama.cpp#20547.title: Feature Request: WebUI: Add "Model Information" button to Models in Router Mode UI

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

When I run llama-completion with the GLM-4-32B model on the Vulkan backend, with flash attention enabled, it gets stuck in a loop, repeating a single character. The issue is specific to this model, specific to Vulkan and only occurs when flash attention is enabled.

With flash attention enabled:

```
$ llama-completion -m GLM-4-32B-0414-Q4_K_S.gguf -c 8192 -t 32 -np 1 -fit off -ngl all -p "Hi there" -st 2>/dev/null

Hi there
Hello?????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????
??????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????????
???????????????????
```

With flash attention disabled:

```
$ llama-completion -m GLM-4-32B-0414-Q4_K_S.gguf -c 8192 -t 32 -np 1 -fit off -ngl all -p "Hi there" -st -fa off 2>/dev/null

Hi there
Hello 👋! I'm ChatGLM（智谱清言）, the artificial intelligence assistant, nice to meet you. Feel free to ask me any questions. [end of text]
```

Other models work fine, and this model works fine with flash attention enabled on ROCm.

### Name and Version

llama-server --version
```shell
ggml_cuda_init: found 2 CUDA devices (Total VRAM: 64217 MiB):
  Device 0: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090, compute capability 12.0, VMM: yes, VRAM: 32105 MiB (31599 MiB free)
  Device 1: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 5090, compute capability 12.0, VMM: yes, VRAM: 32111 MiB (31605 MiB free)
version: 8247 (ae87863dc) # does NOT have issue
version: 8248 (5f4cdac38) # first commit with issue
version: 8352 (d23355afc) # latest still has this issue
built with GNU 13.3.0 for Linux x86_64
```

### Operating systems

Linux

### Which llama.cpp modules do you know to be affected?

llama-server

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

Using llama-server with router mode uses more VRAM than directly loading the model at startup.

Before version: 8248 (5f4cdac38)
I was able to load GPT-OSS 120B into dual 5090s with 62K context with 1 CPU-MoE offloading with or without router mode.
After version: 8248 (5f4cdac38)
Loading GPT-OSS 120B into dual 5090s with 62K context with 1 CPU-MoE offloading with router mode will result in OOM errors.
The context must be reduced to 34K in order to load successfully with router mode.
Without router mode, the model loads successfully with the 62K context with 1 CPU-MoE offloading.

Version: 8248 (5f4cdac38) introduces PR #20185: "cuda : display total and free VRAM capacity during device initialization"

I suspect that the memory usage querying introduced some usage memory and did not free. IDK.

`presets.ini` file
```ini
[gpt-oss-120b-c60k]
m = /home/bobbyl2k/deployment/llama-cpp-deploy/data/gpt-oss-120b-mxfp4-00001-of-00003.gguf
ctx-size = 63488 ; 64K - 2K
temp = 1.0
top-k = 0
top-p = 1.0
n-cpu-moe = 1
gpu-layers = 99
```

### Command line

```shell
# loading model with router, OOM on model load
LLAMA_ARG_MODELS_MAX=1 \
LLAMA_ARG_MODELS_PRESET=presets.ini \
    llama-server
# loading model directly, NO OOM
llama-server \
    --temperature \
    1.0 \
    --top-k \
    0 \
    --top-p \
    1.0 \
    --alias \
    gpt-oss-120b-c60k \
    --ctx-size \
    63488 \
    -m \
    /home/bobbyl2k/deployment/llama-cpp-deploy/data/gpt-oss-120b-mxfp4-00001-of-00003.gguf \
    --n-cpu-moe \
    1 \
    --n-gpu-layers \
    99
```

version: 8248 (5f4cdac38)

ggerganov/llama.cpp#20608.body:
**Platform:** Radxa Orion O6N (aarch64, 32GB unified memory)
**Backend:** CPU only (llama.cpp built from source with ARM optimizations)
**Base commit:** b8352 / d23355afc
**Models tested:** Qwen3.5-9B and Qwen3.5-4B, all using Unsloth UD dynamic quants

---

edit: just tested against master, and this is working now
update: Q8_0 (not dynamic) also fails on Bug 2 below now

~~Bug 1: Crashing unless `auto_fgdn` is force to false~~

---

### Bug 2: `GGML_ASSERT(*cur_backend_id != -1)` crash with certain quant types

**Symptom:**
Even with the `auto_fgdn` workaround above, UD-Q4_K_XL models (both 9B and 4B) crash during `sched_reserve` at context initialization:

```
ggml-backend.cpp:1151: GGML_ASSERT(*cur_backend_id != -1) failed
```

Stack trace:
```
ggml_backend_sched_split_graph+0xb70
ggml_backend_sched_reserve+0x3c
llama_context::graph_reserve
llama_context::sched_reserve
llama_context::llama_context
llama_init_from_model
```

update: the Q4 quants still crash with fgdn enabled now:
```
sched_reserve: resolving fused Gated Delta Net support:
ggml/src/ggml-backend.cpp:1151: GGML_ASSERT(*cur_backend_id != -1) failed
libggml-base.so.0(+0x15384)[0xffff8a955384]
libggml-base.so.0(ggml_print_backtrace+0x1ec)[0xffff8a95575c]
libggml-base.so.0(ggml_abort+0xfc)[0xffff8a9558dc]
libggml-base.so.0(ggml_backend_sched_split_graph+0xb70)[0xffff8a96d480]
libllama.so.0(_ZN13llama_context13graph_reserveEjjjPK22llama_memory_context_ibPm+0x51c)[0xffff8aab515c]
libllama.so.0(_ZN13llama_context13sched_reserveEv+0xd80)[0xffff8aab6dd0]
libllama.so.0(_ZN13llama_contextC2ERK11llama_model20llama_context_params+0x90c)[0xffff8aab98e0]
libllama.so.0(llama_init_from_model+0xd8)[0xffff8aaba948]
llama-server(+0x1a3458)[0xaaaac8c93458]
llama-server(+0x1a3d78)[0xaaaac8c93d78]
llama-server(+0xe1880)[0xaaaac8bd1880]
llama-server(+0x33b80)[0xaaaac8b23b80]
libc.so.6(+0x27744)[0xffff8a4a7744]
libc.so.6(__libc_start_main+0x98)[0xffff8a4a7818]
llama-server(+0x3bb30)[0xaaaac8b2bb30]
```

**Observation:**
Testing the same 4B model, the UD-Q4_K_XL variant crashes while the UD-Q6_K_XL variant works. The Q4 variant contains `iq4_xs` tensors; the Q6 variant does not. Unsloth UD dynamic quants are known to mix in `iq4_xs` tensors on lower-sensitivity layers, which may explain why this only manifests with the Q4 variant.

Crashing (4B UD-Q4_K_XL):
```
f32: 177, f16: 48, q8_0: 24, q4_K: 74, q5_K: 69, q6_K: 24, iq4_xs: 10
```

Working (4B UD-Q6_K_XL):
```
f32: 177, f16: 72, q8_0: 70, q6_K: 108
```

We suspect `iq4_xs` tensors are not handled by the CPU backend on aarch64, causing the scheduler to find no valid backend for those tensors, but have not confirmed this directly.

**Workaround:**
Use UD-Q6_K or other quant variants that do not include `iq4_xs` tensors.

---

### Additional notes

- Bug 1 and Bug 2 are independent — Bug 2 reproduces even with the `auto_fgdn` workaround applied.
- All testing was done with Unsloth UD quants; behavior with non-UD quants is untested.
- We are happy to test patches or provide additional diagnostics on aarch64 hardware.

ggerganov/llama.cpp#20669.title: Compile bug: Build fails on RISC-V without RVV

ggerganov/llama.cpp#20720.body:
### Name and Version

Any version after b8244 has this problem.

### Operating systems

Linux

### GGML backends

CUDA

### Hardware

i9 13900 + 5060 Ti

### Models

Qwen3.5-35B-A3B

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

I tested with OpenCode.

Step to reproduce is you just create a folder of multiple images (6+ images to see this behavior clearly) and ask model to view all of them.

After each image read, the model will back to checkpoint created before read image. This makes the batch image reading very slow.

In b8244
`Prompt1 → [Image 1] → [Image 2] → ... → [Image 10] → Prompt2`
Each prompt and image is processed continuously.

After b8244
`Prompt1 → [Img 1] → [Img 1+2] → ... → [Img 1+2+3+...+10] Prompt2`
It will reprocess every image and prompt after Prompt 1 again. Only Prompt 1 has checkpoint created.

### First Bad Commit

b8245

### Relevant log output

ggerganov/llama.cpp#20736.body:
### Name and Version

version: 8414 (5744d7ec4)
built with GNU 15.2.1 for Linux x86_64

### Operating systems

Linux

### Which llama.cpp modules do you know to be affected?

llama-server

### Command line

```shell
llama-server --port 9292 --context-shift -c 131072
```

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

After enabling an MCP server in the general settings, that choice does not carry over to the next chat. Every time I start a new chat, the MCP server is effectively disabled again and I have to turn it back on manually.

How to reproduce:

1. Open the MCP settings
2. Enable an MCP server
3. Start a new chat
4. Open the MCP selector/settings for that chat
5. The server is no longer enabled for the new chat

Expected behaviour:

I would have expected a server enabled in the general settings to remain enabled by default in new chats, and for chat-specific behavior to only change if I explicitly toggle it for that chat.


https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/eb2e823a-3977-4180-a92a-5300c6eb7e1d

ggerganov/llama.cpp#20883.body:
### Name and Version $ ./build/bin/llama-cli --version ggml_cuda_init: found 2 CUDA devices (Total VRAM: 194500 MiB): Device 0: NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Max-Q Workstation Edition, compute capability 12.0, VMM: yes, VRAM: 97250 MiB Device 1: NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Max-Q Workstation Edition, compute capability 12.0, VMM: yes, VRAM: 97250 MiB version: 8477 (ec2b787eb) built with GNU 15.2.1 for Linux x86_64 ### Operating systems Linux ### Which llama.cpp modules do you know to be affected? llama-server ### Command line I used @ubergarm's llama-swep-bench fork to produce the results: ```shell CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0 ./build/bin/llama-sweep-bench -m /mnt/srv/snowdrift/gguf/Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-GGUF/aes_sedai/Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-Q5_K_M.gguf --ctx-size 65536 --threads 54 --flash-attn 1 --batch-size 4096 --ubatch-size 4096 --fit-target 80000 ``` ### Problem description & steps to reproduce I had received some reports that my Qwen3.5 model quants were performing much slower TG compared to unsloth quants and noticed that I had used the fused up + gate while they didn't. So I produced two identical quantizations, one fused and one unfused, and compared their speeds. When entirely on VRAM, the models have identical TG speeds and the fused model gets about +10% PP speeds. When on VRAM and RAM however, the fused model gets about half the TG as the unfused model. I think this falls into @am17an's bucket so tagging him as an interested party. ### First Bad Commit _No response_ ### Relevant log output
Logs ```console ```

ggerganov/llama.cpp#21091.body:
### Prerequisites

- [x] I am running the latest code. Mention the version if possible as well.
- [x] I carefully followed the [README.md](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/blob/master/README.md).
- [x] I searched using keywords relevant to my issue to make sure that I am creating a new issue that is not already open (or closed).
- [x] I reviewed the [Discussions](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/discussions), and have a new and useful enhancement to share.

### Feature Description

Following the successful ARM64 build fixes in PR https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/pull/20929, we can now restore ARM64 release binaries in the CI workflow.


### Motivation

- Recent fixes have resolved ARM64 build failures
- Providing pre-built ARM64 binaries will improve accessibility for ARM64 users

### Possible Implementation


Restore ARM64 build configuration in `.github/workflows/release.yml` and make corresponding adjustments:

```yaml
  ubuntu-22-cpu:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        include:
          - build: 'x64'
            os: ubuntu-22.04
          - build: 's390x'
            os: ubuntu-24.04-s390x
          # GGML_BACKEND_DL and GGML_CPU_ALL_VARIANTS are not currently supported on arm
          # - build: 'arm64'
          #   os: ubuntu-22.04-arm
```

### Operating systems

Mac

### GGML backends

Metal

### Hardware

Apple Mac Mini M4 Pro 64GB

### Models

MistralAI Voxtral Mini 3B 2507 Q8

https://huggingface.co/bartowski/mistralai_Voxtral-Mini-3B-2507-GGUF


### Problem description & steps to reproduce

The cause of this error is the new code added in `107d599`, it looks like there were issues where the server would get stuck, but in this case the server is busy encoding the audio slices, and after 4 "ticks" of encoding, the kill switch is tripped.

To reproduce, start the server:

```bash
./llama-server
  --model /llm/mistralai_Voxtral-Mini-3B-2507/mistralai_Voxtral-Mini-3B-2507-Q8_0.gguf
  --mmproj /llm/mistralai_Voxtral-Mini-3B-2507/mmproj-mistralai_Voxtral-Mini-3B-2507-f16.gguf
  --ctx-size 16384
  --temp 0.0
  --top-p 0.95
```

Then use the webui to upload an mp3 along with a prompt: `summarize the transcript`. The encoding starts as you can see in the console, but after 4 ticks the kill switch is tripped and the fatal error occurs.

### First Bad Commit

% git bisect bad
107d5999520dd02195ebe05278752db9fd33c865 is the first bad commit
commit 107d5999520dd02195ebe05278752db9fd33c865 (HEAD, tag: b8254)
Author: Georgi Gerganov <ggerganov@gmail.com>
Date:   Mon Mar 9 10:33:12 2026 +0200

    server : add kill switch when server is stuck (#20277)

 tools/server/server-context.cpp | 9 ++++++++-
 1 file changed, 8 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)

As this was my first time using `git bisect`, to confirm I checked out the previous version with `git checkout 107d5999520dd02195ebe05278752db9fd33c865^`, built and confirmed that `8253 (e8bbc736c)` is the last good version and 8254 (107d59995) is the first bad one.

ggerganov/llama.cpp#21162.body:
### Name and Version

./bin/llama-cli --version
ggml_cuda_init: found 1 CUDA devices (Total VRAM: 97247 MiB):
  Device 0: NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Max-Q Workstation Edition, compute capability 12.0, VMM: yes, VRAM: 97247 MiB
version: 8580 (7c203670f)
built with GNU 13.3.0 for Linux x86_64

### Operating systems

Linux

### Which llama.cpp modules do you know to be affected?

Other (Please specify in the next section)

### Command line

```shell
./bin/test-backend-ops -o TOP_K
```

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

How to reproduce the problem:

**Step 1**: Apply the following patch to test-backend-ops.cpp:
```
diff --git a/tests/test-backend-ops.cpp b/tests/test-backend-ops.cpp
index 6a4f9b634..096ec58b1 100644
--- a/tests/test-backend-ops.cpp
+++ b/tests/test-backend-ops.cpp
@@ -8461,6 +8461,10 @@ static std::vector<std::unique_ptr<test_case>> make_test_cases_eval() {
     //    test_cases.emplace_back(new test_top_k(GGML_TYPE_F32, {i, 2, 1, 3}, rand() % i + 1));
     //}
 
+    test_cases.emplace_back(new test_top_k(GGML_TYPE_F32, {512, 512, 1, 1}, 512));
+    test_cases.emplace_back(new test_top_k(GGML_TYPE_F32, {1024, 512, 1, 1}, 1024));
+    test_cases.emplace_back(new test_top_k(GGML_TYPE_F32, {1536, 512, 1, 1}, 1536));
+
     for (ggml_scale_mode mode : {GGML_SCALE_MODE_NEAREST, GGML_SCALE_MODE_BILINEAR, GGML_SCALE_MODE_BICUBIC, ggml_scale_mode(GGML_SCALE_MODE_BILINEAR | GGML_SCALE_FLAG_ANTIALIAS)}) {
         test_cases.emplace_back(new test_upscale(GGML_TYPE_F32, {512, 512, 3, 2}, 2, mode));
         test_cases.emplace_back(new test_upscale(GGML_TYPE_F32, {512, 512, 3, 2}, 2, mode, true));
```

**Step 2**: Make a CUDA build of llama.cpp. CUDA version I used:

```
nvcc --version
nvcc: NVIDIA (R) Cuda compiler driver
Copyright (c) 2005-2025 NVIDIA Corporation
Built on Fri_Feb_21_20:23:50_PST_2025
Cuda compilation tools, release 12.8, V12.8.93
Build cuda_12.8.r12.8/compiler.35583870_0
```

**Step 3**: Run TOP_K op tests:

```
./bin/test-backend-ops -o TOP_K
```

**Observed result**: Test crashes
```
ggml_backend_cuda_graph_compute: CUDA graph warmup reset
  TOP_K(type=f32,ne=[512,512,1,1],k=512,ties=0): OK
ggml_backend_cuda_graph_compute: CUDA graph warmup complete
  TOP_K(type=f32,ne=[1024,512,1,1],k=1024,ties=0): OK
CUDA error: an illegal memory access was encountered
  current device: 0, in function ggml_backend_cuda_synchronize at /home/phm/projects/llama.cpp/ggml/src/ggml-cuda/ggml-cuda.cu:2924
```

Full logs below.

**Expected result**: Test finishes successfully

**Rationale**: #21149 needs this working for tensor shapes up to `{context size, ubatch size, 1, B}`.

**Note**: Everything works correctly if you build llama.cpp with NVIDIA CUDA CCCL library (version >3.2) and enabled GGML_CUDA_USE_CUB. Tested on CUDA 13.2 and CCCL 13.2.27.

### First Bad Commit

No idea if this ever worked correctly for tensors this big.

### Relevant log output

<details>
<summary>Logs</summary>
<!-- Copy-pasted short logs go into the "console" area here -->

```console
$ ./bin/test-backend-ops -o TOP_K
```

ggerganov/llama.cpp#21162.title: Misc. bug: CUDA ggml_top_k() implementation crashes for large tensor shapes

ggerganov/llama.cpp#21167.body:
### Name and Version

```
$ llama-server --version
version: 8577 (2405d59cb)
built with GNU 13.3.0 for Linux x86_64
```

### Operating systems

Linux

### Which llama.cpp modules do you know to be affected?

llama-server

### Command line

```shell
llama-server --metrics -np 1 --jinja --cache-prompt --host 127.0.0.1 --offline -ctk q8_0 -ctv q8_0 -c 0 --webui-mcp-proxy --port 11435 --api-key-file /path/to/api.key --models-max 2
```

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

When llama-server is started with both `--api-key-file` and `--webui-mcp-proxy`, the `/cors-proxy` endpoint is subject to the global API key validation middleware. The web UI does not automatically include the API key in its requests to `/cors-proxy`, so MCP server connections fail with 401.

**Steps to reproduce:**

1. Start llama-server with `--api-key-file <file> --webui-mcp-proxy`
2. Authenticate with the web UI using the API key
3. Add an MCP server (e.g. `http://127.0.0.1:8581/mcp`) with "Use Proxy" enabled
4. Attempt to connect — connection fails with:
   `Streamable HTTP error: Error POSTing to endpoint: {"error":{"message":"Invalid API Key","type":"authentication_error","code":401}}`

**Expected behavior:** The web UI already has the API key (the user authenticated to use the UI), so CORS proxy requests should automatically include it.

**Workaround:** Manually add `Authorization: Bearer <api-key>` as a custom header on each MCP server entry.

### First Bad Commit

_No response_

### Relevant log output

<details>
<summary>Logs</summary>

```console
Connection failed: NetworkError when attempting to fetch resource.
Streamable HTTP error: Error POSTing to endpoint: {"error":{"message":"Invalid API Key","type":"authentication_error","code":401}}
```
</details>
ggerganov/llama.cpp#21167.title: Misc. bug: WebUI CORS proxy requests don't include API key for MCP connections

Something has been introduced in b8579 that crashes Qwen3.5-35B-A3B on my Linux 2xRadeon Pro VII ROCm setup when MoE layers are loaded into the GPU. b8578 runs normally, but with b8579 I get an error about MUL_MAT_ID failing when I enter my first prompt. b8578->b8579 is only the commit ec16a072f06c9c44d33513405a83068b15ae1b2c, which deals with MUL_MAT_ID stuff. * TheRock-7.13 build from https://therock-nightly-tarball.s3.amazonaws.com/therock-dist-linux-gfx906-7.13.0a20260325.tar.gz

My build is configured as following: 

```
HIPCXX="$(hipconfig -l)/clang" HIP_PATH="$(hipconfig -R)" \
```

The model runs, wenn -cmoe is used instead.

ggerganov/llama.cpp#21229.body:
### Name and Version

$ llama-server --version
version: 8604 (6307ec07d)
built with GNU 13.3.0 for Linux x86_64

### Operating systems

Linux

### Which llama.cpp modules do you know to be affected?

llama-server

### Command line

```shell
#!/usr/bin/env bash

LLAMA_SERVER="${HOME}/ml/git/github.com/llama.cpp/build/bin/llama-server"

# Get the number of physical cores (not logical cores!)
PHY_CORES=$(lscpu | awk '/^Core\(s\) per socket:/{cores=$NF} /^Socket\(s\):/{sockets=$NF} END{print cores*sockets}')

LLM_PARAMS=(
  --host $(hostname -I | awk '{ print $1 }')
  --port 7860
  --api-key-file ${HOME}/ml/etc/api-keys.txt
  --models-preset ${HOME}/ml/etc/llama-config.ini
  --models-max 1
  --parallel 1
  --threads ${PHY_CORES}
  --threads-batch ${PHY_CORES}
  --mmap
  --no-direct-io
  --numa distribute
  --cache-prompt
  --webui-mcp-proxy
)

"${LLAMA_SERVER}" "${LLM_PARAMS[@]}" 2>&1 | tee "${HOME}/ml/log/llama-server.log.$(date "+%Y-%b-%d_%H:%M:%S")"
```

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

I woke up this morning, compiled the latest Github build, and suddenly all of my browsers on multiple computers which use the llama-server webui are coming up completely blank.   No errors, nothing.

Looking at the llama-server log, I see:

```
srv  log_server_r: done request: GET / 192.168.1.101 200
Unauthorized: Invalid API Key
```

Something appears to have changed with API key handling overnight.   Users do not have the option of reentering their API key because the web browser returns a blank screen.


### First Bad Commit

Appears to be something committed after b8591.

### Relevant log output

srv  log_server_r: done request: GET / 192.168.1.101 200
Unauthorized: Invalid API Key
ggerganov/llama.cpp#21229.title: Sudden "Unauthorized: Invalid API Key" with this morning's build

ggml-org/ggml#1450.body:
I've encountered a strange issue that I can only now reproduce.

In the Debian CI, we run `test-backend-ops` for all backends that we build. On the amd64 workers, the CPU backend started failing (via timeout) consistently in the CI. Some digging revealed that the process was in fact sleeping, deadlocked on `semget(2)`.

Some more digging revealed that the `RWKV_WKV6` test deadlocks on machines with 33+ threads, like our amd64 worker has. That's why I could never reproduce it on any of my local machines.

Steps to reproduce below. I could reproduce this in a QEMU VM: boot it with 32 cores and everything runs fine, boot it with 33 cores and the problem appears. I believe same should be achievable on a high-core machine by fiddling with the `N_THREADS` macro in `test-backend-ops.cpp`.

```
$ cmake -B build && cmake --build build
$ build/bin/test-backend-ops -b CPU -o RWKV_WKV6
```

In a VM running Debian unstable with 32 threads, this runs fine (ignore the CPU name, QEMU just passed in the host CPU name to the guest):

```
Testing 1 devices
 
Backend 1/1: CPU
  Device description: AMD Ryzen 9 9950X 16-Core Processor
  Device memory: 32093 MB (32093 MB free)
 
  RWKV_WKV6(type=f32,head_count=32,head_size=64,n_seq_tokens=1,n_seqs=1): OK
  RWKV_WKV6(type=f32,head_count=32,head_size=64,n_seq_tokens=32,n_seqs=1): OK
  RWKV_WKV6(type=f32,head_count=32,head_size=64,n_seq_tokens=32,n_seqs=4): OK
  RWKV_WKV6(type=f32,head_count=32,head_size=64,n_seq_tokens=128,n_seqs=4): OK
  4/4 tests passed
  Backend CPU: OK
1/1 backends passed
OK
```

With 33 threads, this just hangs:

```
Testing 1 devices
 
Backend 1/1: CPU
  Device description: AMD Ryzen 9 9950X 16-Core Processor
  Device memory: 32093 MB (32093 MB free)
```

I haven't tested this on other architectures or other OSes yet. I first verified this in an AWS r7i with 96 vCPUS.
ggml-org/ggml#1450.title: CPU backend: test-backend-ops deadlocks on RWKV_WKV6 with 33+ threads

ggml-org/llama.cpp#14909.body:
### Prerequisites

- [x] I am running the latest code. Mention the version if possible as well.
- [x] I carefully followed the [README.md](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/blob/master/README.md).
- [x] I searched using keywords relevant to my issue to make sure that I am creating a new issue that is not already open (or closed).
- [x] I reviewed the [Discussions](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/discussions), and have a new and useful enhancement to share.

### Feature Description

The list of supported operation in ggml per backend is maintained at https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/blob/master/docs/ops.md

How to contribute to make the list more complete:

1. Pick an operation which is not implemented in a backend (pick ones marked with ❌), pick only one per PR
2. Implement the operation
3. Test your implementation with `test-backend-ops -o <op>`
4. If it passes test for the op, create a PR.
5. Mention performance tests in the PR if they exist (via `test-backend-ops perf -o <op>`)

Notes:
1. The ops suffixed with _BACK are related to training which is WIP and does not require immediate attention
2. Do not link this issue in your PR, which will lead to closing this issue when your PR is merged. Instead, just mention it. Example PRs will be linked below
3. This list can be temporarily out of sync, you can check support by first running `test-backend-ops -o <op>` on master to confirm 
4. Please do not ask to be assigned a particular task, make the PR first if you think it will be useful


### Motivation

Feature parity between backends is always desirable and it's easy to test the implementation, making it an ideal good first issue.

### Possible Implementation

_No response_

### Name and Version

version: 7220 (ecf74a841)

### Operating systems

Linux

### Which llama.cpp modules do you know to be affected?

Other (Please specify in the next section)

### Command line

```shell
llama-server -m qwen3_next_80b_a30b_instruct-iq4_nl.gguf --jinja --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8357 -c 60000 --cpu-moe
```

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

The new WebUI when running the server in single-model mode shows the model as "gpt-3.5-turbo".

I guess the reason for this is that by default, that's what the OpenAI client sends out as message.model and the new code has:

```typescript
	let displayedModel = $derived((): string | null => {
		if (!currentConfig.showModelInfo) return null;

		if (message.model) {
			return message.model;
		}

		return serverModel;
	});
```

so it always prioritizes the model from the message, even if the model is bogus.

### Models

Same behavior observed on all quants I tested
1. lmstudio-community/Qwen3-Coder-Next-GGUF @ Q4_K_M and Q8_0
2. Qwen/Qwen3-Coder-Next-GGUF @ Q4_K_M


### Problem description & steps to reproduce

Qwen3-Coder-Next gets tripped up on "syntax issues" when run in `llama-server`. I run the attached [prompt.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25057959/prompt.txt), which asks if `server.cpp` has any syntax errors.

This specific prompt is not the most "realistic," but it clearly demonstrates an issue I see when I ask the model to generally "review the changes in file X." It smells to me like an implementation bug, given that the output is noticeably worse in llama-server at both 4 bit and 8 bit quants compared to vLLM running FP8 or MLX running int 4.

llama-server Outputs:
- [llama-server-out.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25057981/llama-server-out.txt): uses lmstudio-community/Qwen3-Coder-Next-GGUF @ Q4_K_M. Shows poor output with misidentified syntax errors, seemingly stumbling over itself.
- [llama-server-out-q8.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25057984/llama-server-out-q8.txt): uses lmstudio-community/Qwen3-Coder-Next-GGUF @ Q8_0 . Shows similarly poor outputs to Q4.
- [llama-server-out-qwen-quant.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25057991/llama-server-out-qwen-quant.txt): Uses Qwen/Qwen3-Coder-Next-GGUF @ Q4_K_M . Shows similar outputs to the lmstudio-community quants.

Outputs from other engines:
- [vllm-fp8.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25058028/vllm-fp8.txt) : vLLM running the Qwen/Qwen3-Coder-Next-FP8 model. Does not report any incorrect errors.
- [lms-mlx-engine-out.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25058016/lms-mlx-engine-out.txt): mlx-engine running the lmstudio-community/Qwen3-Coder-Next-MLX-4bit model LM Studio. Does report any incorrect errors.

### First Bad Commit

I did not observe a commit where this was previously working.

ggml-org/llama.cpp#19305.title: Eval bug: Qwen3-Coder-Next Poor Outputs

ggml-org/llama.cpp#19335.body:
### Prerequisites

- [x] I am running the latest code. Mention the version if possible as well.
- [x] I carefully followed the [README.md](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/blob/master/README.md).
- [x] I searched using keywords relevant to my issue to make sure that I am creating a new issue that is not already open (or closed).
- [x] I reviewed the [Discussions](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/discussions), and have a new and useful enhancement to share.

### Feature Description

Hi team, pinging @ngxson,
I would like to request support for integrating the GLM-OCR model into llama.cpp.
GLM-OCR is an open-source multilingual OCR model published on Hugging Face:
[🔗 https://huggingface.co/zai-org/GLM-OCR](https://huggingface.co/zai-org/GLM-OCR)

The enhancement requested:
Allow loading and running GLM-OCR using llama.cpp or ggml-compatible formats.
Provide support for text extraction (OCR) inference via existing or extended pipelines.
Optional: Include sample usage scripts demonstrating how to run GLM-OCR within the llama.cpp framework.
This feature would significantly extend the usability of llama.cpp beyond language models, enabling OCR tasks using the same efficient CPU/GPU inference backend.

### Motivation

GLM-OCR provides strong multilingual OCR capabilities and is useful for applications involving text recognition from images. Adding support in llama.cpp would:
Enable users to run OCR tasks fully offline with high efficiency
Expand the diversity of model types supported by ggml/llama.cpp
Make it easier to build integrated pipelines using both LLMs and OCR in a single unified runtime
Support more community projects that require lightweight OCR models without depending on cloud APIs
This aligns with llama.cpp’s goal of enabling fast, portable AI inference on local devices.

### Possible Implementation

Some possible directions:
Convert GLM-OCR into a ggml/gguf-compatible format, similar to how vision or multimodal models are handled.
Extend the model loader to support GLM-OCR’s architecture (if it diverges from existing supported ones).
ggml-org/llama.cpp#19335.title: Feature Request: Add Support for GLM-OCR Model

ggml-org/llama.cpp#19543.body:
### Name and Version

❯ ./llama-server --version
version: 8007 (4ae1b7517)
built with IntelLLVM 2025.3.0 for Linux x86_64

### Operating systems

Linux

### Which llama.cpp modules do you know to be affected?

llama-server

### Command line

```shell
./llama-server -m ~/Downloads/models/gguf/qwen-3-vl-8b/Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct-Q6_K.gguf --mmproj ~/Downloads/models/gguf/qwen-3-vl-8b/mmproj-BF16.gguf -ngl 999 -c 4096 -n 512 -fa on --host 0.0.0.0 --no-warmup --image-max-tokens 2048 -np 1
```

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

When running Qwen3-VL, the vision encoder fails to use SYCL acceleration for several key operators, leading to CPU fallback and slow performance (~23s for image encoding). The logs explicitly list unsupported ops: UPSCALE, SOFT_MAX, ROPE, MUL_MAT, etc.

### First Bad Commit

_No response_

### Reference
This relates to **PR #16837** (SYCL support for Qwen-VL). The log includes a direct reference to it.
ggml-org/llama.cpp#19543.title: Misc. bug:  [SYCL] Missing kernels for Qwen3-VL vision encoder (UPSCALE, mRoPE, etc.) on Intel Arc A770

ggml-org/llama.cpp#19653.body:
### Name and Version

build: 8055 (079feab9e) with GNU 11.4.0 for Linux x86_64
system info: n_threads = 12, n_threads_batch = 12, total_threads = 12


### Operating systems

Linux

### Which llama.cpp modules do you know to be affected?

Documentation/Github

### Command line

```shell
services:
  qwen3coder:
    image: ghcr.io/ggml-org/llama.cpp:server-cuda12-b8055
    container_name: qwen3coder
    deploy:
      resources:
        reservations:
          devices:
            - driver: nvidia
              capabilities: [gpu]
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    volumes:
      - /home/slavik/.cache/llama.cpp/router/local-qwen3-coder80b:/root/.cache/llama.cpp
    entrypoint: ["./llama-server"]
    command: >
      --model  /root/.cache/llama.cpp/Qwen_Qwen3-Coder-Next-GGUF_Qwen3-Coder-Next-Q6_K_Qwen3-Coder-Next-Q6_K-00001-of-00004.gguf
      --alias local-qwen3-coder80b
      --host 0.0.0.0  --port 8080
      --ctx-size 196608
      --parallel 2  --kv-unified
      --top-p 0.95 --top-k 40 --temp 1.0 --min-p 0.01
      --verbose-prompt
      --log-verbosity
      --log-timestamps
```

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

Looking at the doc here:
https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/blob/master/tools/server/README.md

said:

--verbose-prompt	print a verbose prompt before generation (default: false)

--log-verbosity N	Set the verbosity threshold. 

--log-timestamps	Enable timestamps in log messages

None of these options working in the server.
May be they work in CLI, not sure.


### First Bad Commit

_No response_

ggml-org/llama.cpp#19653.title: Misc. bug: verbose logging doesn't work for llama server

ggml-org/llama.cpp#19779.body:
### Name and Version

I guess all versions after 8052 version. I haven't tested all versions, but all builds below have the simillar issue.

**Latest succeeded version:**
8052 (b7742cf32) built with Clang 19.1.5 for Windows x86_64

**llama.cpp server boot failed:**
llama-b8095-bin-win-sycl-x64
llama-b8083-bin-win-sycl-x64
llama-b8071-bin-win-sycl-x64
llama-b8058-bin-win-sycl-x64
llama-b8053-bin-win-sycl-x64

**llama.cpp server boot successed, but chat failed:**
llama-b8121-bin-win-sycl-x64
llama-b8119-bin-win-sycl-x64
llama-b8118-bin-win-sycl-x64
llama-b8117-bin-win-sycl-x64
llama-b8110-bin-win-sycl-x64 
llama-b8099-bin-win-sycl-x64
llama-b8098-bin-win-sycl-x64

### Operating systems

Windows

### GGML backends

SYCL

### Hardware

AMD Ryzen 7600 + Intel Arc A770 16GB + 32GB RAM.

### Models

[Qwen3-Coder-Next-Q3_K_M.gguf by Unsloth](https://huggingface.co/unsloth/Qwen3-Coder-Next-GGUF)

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

I downloaded [Unsloth's Qwen3-Coder-Next-Q3_K_M.gguf](https://huggingface.co/unsloth/Qwen3-Coder-Next-GGUF) file and used it on my llama.cpp server to see how fast the quantized Qwen3 Coder Next would run on my computer. Until last week, it was running fine, albeit slowly. However, after updating to the latest llama.cpp build, shortly after sending the first chat message, the server crashed with the following message:

> Server Error
> The server responded with an error message. Review the details below.
> network error

And the following error remained in the log:

> D:\a\llama.cpp\llama.cpp\ggml\src\ggml-sycl\binbcast.cpp:200: GGML_ASSERT(s10 == 1) failed

I'm not sure if it will help, but I've included the contents of the PowerShell script I used to get the server up and running below.

<details>
<summary>PowerShell Script</summary>

```console
$LlamaRoot   = "D:\llama"
$ModelPath   = "D:\llama\model\Qwen3-Coder-Next-Q3_K_M.gguf"
$Port        = 8000
$Threads     = 10
$NGpuLayers  = 32

$procs = Get-Process -Name "llama-server" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
if ($procs) {
    Write-Host "Shutting down the existing llama-server..."
    $procs | Stop-Process -Force
}

$LlamaServer = Join-Path $LlamaRoot "llama-server.exe"

Write-Host "llama.cpp server running..."
Write-Host "Model: $ModelPath"
Write-Host "Number of GPU layers: $NGpuLayers"
Write-Host "Port: $Port"
Write-Host "Context: 6144"
Write-Host ""

& $LlamaServer `
  --model $ModelPath `
  --port $Port `
  --threads $Threads `
  --ctx-size 6144 `
  --parallel 1 `
  --batch-size 1024 `
  --ubatch-size 512 `
  --n-gpu-layers $NGpuLayers `
  --cache-ram 1024 `
  --fit off `
  --jinja `
  --temp 1.0 `
  --top-p 0.95 `
  --min-p 0.01 `
  --top-k 40 `
  --seed (Get-Random -Minimum 0 -Maximum 4294967295)

Write-Host "`n✅ Server boot complete! http://localhost:$Port/chat"
```

</details>

If this issue is already known or if I didn't explain it properly. I apologize.

### First Bad Commit

I suspect the 8053 version is the first one to experience the boot failed problem.
And 8098 version is the first one to experience the server boot successed, but chat failed.

### Relevant log output

<details>
<summary>Logs</summary>
<!-- Copy-pasted short logs go into the "console" area here -->

ggml-org/llama.cpp#19779.title: Eval bug: binbcast.cpp:200: GGML_ASSERT(s10 == 1) failed and Server down.

ggml-org/llama.cpp#19886.body:
### Name and Version

version: 8149 (a96a1120)
built with IntelLLVM 2025.3.0 for Linux x86_64

### Operating systems

Linux

### GGML backends

SYCL

### Hardware

Intel® Core™ i5-1340P

### Models

gpt-oss-20b-mxfp4.gguf

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

I use a slightly edited version of the [dockerfile](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/blob/a96a1120b45c011ef6ed38bcb3fc455209ea5b9e/.devops/intel.Dockerfile) to build llama.cpp.

After running below command (and wait a few seconds), the error message will be printed and llama.cpp exits instantly.

```sh
docker run --rm -v /home/user/LLM_models:/models \
--device /dev/dri/renderD128:/dev/dri/renderD128 \
--device /dev/dri/card1:/dev/dri/card1\
 image_name /app/llama-bench --model '/models/gpt-oss-20b-mxfp4.gguf' -ngl 0
```

#### error message

```
The number of work-items in each dimension of a work-group cannot exceed {512, 512, 512} for this deviceException caught at file:/app/ggml/src/ggml-sycl/ggml-sycl.cpp, line:4167
Error OP ADD_ID
```

After searching the error message on GitHub, I blindly copy-pasted the change in #14732 to the file add-id.cpp as the diff below and llama.cpp runs fine without the error.

```diff
diff --git a/ggml/src/ggml-sycl/add-id.cpp b/ggml/src/ggml-sycl/add-id.cpp
index 00c073cf..ac32697d 100644
--- a/ggml/src/ggml-sycl/add-id.cpp
+++ b/ggml/src/ggml-sycl/add-id.cpp
@@ -55,7 +55,12 @@ void ggml_sycl_add_id(ggml_backend_sycl_context& ctx, ggml_tensor* dst) {
   const int32_t* src2_d = (const int32_t*)src2->data;
   float* dst_d = (float*)dst->data;
 
-  int threads = std::min((int)ne00, 768);  // cols
```

ggml-org/llama.cpp#20417.body:
Currently running pyright on `convert_hf_to_gguf.py` produces 30 warnings making it difficult to use it locally. 

I think we should try to fix these or add exclusions for the ones that perhaps can't be fixed so that pyright can be run locally. My understanding of the CI job is that it only checks the diff of the PR and not the whole file which is the reason that we don't see the errors in CI that we do locally.

ggml-org/llama.cpp#20423.body:
I am trying to run [Jackrong/Qwen3.5-9B-Claude-4.6-Opus-Reasoning-Distilled-GGUF](https://huggingface.co/Jackrong/Qwen3.5-9B-Claude-4.6-Opus-Reasoning-Distilled-GGUF) (Q4_K_M) on an Intel Arc A770 using the SYCL backend.

**On the latest release (b8277),** the model fails to start with `llama-server` unless Flash Attention is explicitly disabled (`-fa 0`). Without this flag, slot initialization hangs indefinitely.

**After building from master** (which includes the fix from #20283), the situation improves. The model starts and produces some good answers. However, without that merged PR, the output is pure gibberish and repetition loops — for example, endlessly repeating "The question asks: Let me analyze this question carefully."

**The remaining problem:** Even with the latest master build and `-fa 0`, the server becomes unstable after a few successful responses. It starts producing gibberish again — sometimes unicode garbage mixed with random multilingual text fragments, returning HTTP 500 errors. Once this happens, subsequent requests also fail until the server is restarted.

The server log shows:
```
ched_reserve: reserving ...
sched_reserve: Flash Attention was auto, set to enabled
sched_reserve: layer 0 is assigned to device SYCL0 but the fused Gated Delta Net tensor is assigned to device CPU (usually due to missing support)
sched_reserve: fused Gated Delta Net not supported, set to disabled
```

This suggests the fused Gated Delta Net kernel is not implemented for the SYCL backend, and the fallback path is unreliable.

Related issue: #20321

**Hardware:** Intel Arc A770 16GB
**OS:** Linux (Ubuntu)
**Backend:** SYCL
ggml-org/llama.cpp#20423.title: SYCL: Qwen3.5 produces gibberish and crashes on Intel Arc A770

ggml-org/llama.cpp#20541.body:
### Prerequisites

- [x] I am running the latest code. Mention the version if possible as well.
- [x] I carefully followed the [README.md](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/blob/master/README.md).
- [x] I searched using keywords relevant to my issue to make sure that I am creating a new issue that is not already open (or closed).
- [x] I reviewed the [Discussions](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/discussions), and have a new and useful enhancement to share.

### Feature Description

Control Vectors are currently missing for Qwen3.5, Qwen3.5-moe and Qwen3-coder-next.
I use these models a lot and would love to see cvector support for them.

Currently an attempt to create a control vector crashes and trying to use one does nothing.

### Motivation

Feature is present in Qwen3-vl and other models so it ideally should work across newer Qwen models. 
(I'd say across all models but I'll argue for the ones I can test and actually use here.)

### Possible Implementation

As far as I can see, it would be as easy as adding

```cpp
cur = build_cvec(cur, il);
cb(cur, "l_out", il);
```

to `src/models/qwen35.cpp`, `src/models/qwen35moe.cpp` and `src/models/qwen3next.cpp` in the llm_build method before `// Input for next layer`. 

This is how it is implemented for other models. I tested and built it locally and it seems to work for me.
But this is the first time I look into that code and I'm unsure if there is other side effects that I didn't see.
ggml-org/llama.cpp#20541.title: Feature Request: Control vector support for Qwen3.5, Qwen3.5-moe and Qwen3-next

ggml-org/llama.cpp#20684.body:
### Name and Version

$ ./bin/llama-cli --version
version: 8391 (740a447fc)
built with GNU 13.3.0 for Linux x86_64


### Operating systems

Linux

### Which llama.cpp modules do you know to be affected?

llama-server

### Command line

```shell
bin/llama-server -m gpt-oss-20b-mxfp4.gguf -ub 2048 -b 2048 --host 0.0.0.0
```

### Problem description & steps to reproduce

We are deploying `llama-server` in a k8s environment where the health status of the pod is periodically checked via the `/health` endpoint:
```
    Liveness:   http-get http://:3080/health delay=0s timeout=1s period=15s #success=1 #failure=1
```

I am observing a lot of restarts of the `llama-server` pod which probably means that `/health` fails to return `HTTP 200 OK` response in the 1 second timeout.

Looking at the how HTTP requests are handled in `llama-server`, I don't see any special treatment for `/health` requests which means they are queued when the server is under heavy load like all other requests. I think we should change this and add special handling for `/health` which guarantees that it will promptly return healthy status, even when the server is under heavy load.

### First Bad Commit

_No response_

### Relevant log output

_No response_
ggml-org/llama.cpp#20684.title: Misc. bug: llama-server should have special handling for /health
